checks.a_analysis: I have reviewed the visible sections of the output JSON (Title, Overview, Why use SharePoint Online, Conclusion, Thank you) and compared them against the corresponding pages in the source PDF. All text content in these sections is directly derived from the source. The placeholder for omitted sections is a system-generated structural element and does not constitute fabrication of source content.

The source PDF contains a URL (https://lookbook.microsoft.com) on several slides. This URL is not present in the visible portions of the JSON, as the slides containing it (15-19) are part of the omitted block. No other contact information exists in the source. No fabricated phone numbers, emails, or URLs were found in the visible output.

The output JSON accounts for all 21 pages of the source PDF. Pages 1-3 and 20-21 are explicitly provided, while the 16 pages in between (4-19) are noted as omitted due to token budget constraints. The count of omitted sections (16) matches the number of slides between the 'Why use SharePoint Online' slide and the 'Conclusion' slide.
The source document is a presentation slide deck and does not contain any tables. Therefore, this check is not applicable.

checks.check_name: Significant text omission within a section
checks.a_analysis: I compared the text within the visible sections of the JSON to the source PDF. The 'Overview', 'Why use SharePoint Online', 'Conclusion', and 'Thank you' sections contain the full text of the corresponding slides with no omissions. All content items from the source pages are preserved in the output elements.

checks.check_name: Minor text differences beyond acceptable reformatting
checks.a_analysis: The text in the visible sections of the JSON is a verbatim representation of the text in the source PDF. Punctuation, capitalization, and phrasing remain unchanged (e.g., 'M365 SharePoint Online seamlessly integrates...' matches page 3 exactly).

checks.a_analysis: I examined the provided sections of the JSON output and compared them with the source PDF images and OCR. I found no instances where text blocks or sections were duplicated in the output that appear only once in the source.

The heading hierarchy is consistent throughout the document. Each page title is assigned an H1 level, and sub-sections or points within those pages are assigned H2 levels. There are no instances of skipped levels (e.g., H1 to H3).
The source document contains no tables; therefore, no table structures were evaluated or found to be corrupted.
The reading order in the JSON output follows the logical slide-by-slide progression of the source PDF. Content within each section is ordered correctly according to the visual layout of the slides.

Several pages in the source document contain bulleted lists (pages 2, 3, 6, 14, 20, 21). In the output, these lists are not represented using a list structure. Instead, they are converted into a flat sequence of headings and paragraphs (e.g., on pages 2 and 3) or simply as paragraphs (e.g., on page 21). This results in a loss of the semantic list structure defined in the source.
checks.b_evidence_source: The source PDF on pages 2, 3, 20, and 21 uses bullet points (represented by triangle icons) to organize information.
checks.b_evidence_output: The JSON output for these pages (Sections 1, 2, 19, 20) does not contain 'list' elements, but instead uses 'heading' (level 2) and 'paragraph' types.

checks.a_analysis: On page 21 of the source document, the text 'Microsoft Copilot' is formatted as an external link (blue, underlined). In the JSON output, this link is missing and is represented as plain text within a paragraph.
checks.b_evidence_output: Section 20 (Page 21), reading_order 134: 'Most images you saw in this presentation... were created by Microsoft Copilot' (plain text, where bold/italic/underline/code are false)
